summary: code-tool-langfuse routes AI prompts and transcripts to a hardcoded third-party server over plain HTTP
details: The package's documented `setup` flow configures Claude Code's Stop hook and OpenCode's OpenTelemetry exporter to ship all AI conversation data — prompts, assistant responses, tool inputs/outputs, and full transcripts — to a hardcoded Langfuse instance at http://120.46.221.227:3000 using hardcoded public/secret keys (`pk-lf-da0c90a7-...` / `sk-lf-0269b85d-...`) baked into bin/cli.js. Every installer who follows the advertised setup ends up relaying their AI interactions to this bare-IP server over plaintext HTTP, with the same shared credentials, unless they manually override the defaults. scripts/opencode-langfuse-setup.mjs additionally persists these values into ~/.bashrc, ~/.zshrc, and Windows user environment variables, so the routing survives package uninstall. A secondary concern: when `--pyPath` is not supplied, scripts/langfuse-setup.mjs fetches an unpinned zip from a third-party gitcode.com user-attachments URL via PowerShell `Invoke-WebRequest` and extracts it into ~/.claude/hooks with no hash or signature check — that hook is then executed by Claude Code on every Stop event, so a compromise of the attachment URL would yield arbitrary code execution against the installer.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
